coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric dual-emission of Rhodamine-B grafted carbon dots for full-range solvent components detection
coredata.dc:creator: He, Qian
Zhuang, Shengyi
Yu, Yuxiu
Li, Haojie
Liu, Yaodong
coredata.dc:description: Quick and visual detection of component contents, such as water, in a mixed solvent is important for many practical applications, and a full range detection is especially preferred. In this work, a carbon dots based ratiometric fluorescent sensor was synthesized by grafting fluorescent group (Rhodamine B, RhB) on carbon dots, and the dual emission peaks exhibited a linear ratiometric response with the change of polarity and hydrogen bond of Solvent Hansen solubility parameters. This responsive behavior is attributed to surface state photoluminescence mechanisms, and has been used for the quantitative detection of water content in ethanol with an excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.996), a low detection limit (0.2%), and a full detection range (0–100%). Furthermore, a paper-based ratiometric fluorescence sensing strip is also demonstrated, which exhibits good storage stability and sensitivity. This study suggests that RhB grafted carbon dots could be feasibly and effectively used as ratiometric fluorescent sensors for solvent content detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrothermal
Carbon dots
Hansen solubility parameter
Dual emission
Ratiometric sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support by One Hundred Person Project of the Shanxi province, Science Foundation of Shanxi Province , China (Grant No. 201903D121006 and 201901D111006ZD ).
The authors acknowledge the financial support by One Hundred Person Project of the Shanxi province, Science Foundation of Shanxi Province, China (Grant No. 201903D121006 and 201901D111006ZD).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XPS survey spectrum; fine spectra of (b) C1s, and (c) O1s of CDs; (d) FTIR spectra; (e) XPS survey spectrum, fine spectra of (f) C1s, (g) O1s, and (h) N1s of CDs-EDA; (i) XPS survey spectrum, fine spectra of (j) C1s, (k) O1s, and (l) N1s of CDs-RhB.

TEM images of (a) CDs and (e) CDs-RhB (insert: upper left - HRTEM, bottom left - optical images without and with 355 nm irradiation); Size distribution histogram of (b) CDs and (f) CDs-RhB; PL spectra of (c) CDs and (g) CDs-RhB excited at 320, 340, 360, 380, and 400 nm; (d) Absorption spectra of CDs (0.1 mg/mL) and CDs-RhB (0.1 mg/mL); (h) Zeta potentials of CDs and CDs-RhB.

PL spectra of CDs-RhB excited at 320, 340, 360, 380, and 400 nm in (a) H2O, (b) DMSO; (c) PL spectra of the mixtures of H2O and DMSO (λex = 400 nm), the legend represents the hydrogen-bonding parameter of mixtures; (d) The relationship between solvent hydrogen-bonding parameter and IG/IRhB. PL spectra of CDs-RhB excited at 320, 340, 360, 380, and 400 nm in (e) DPG, (f) TEG, and (g) EtOH; (h) PL spectra of the mixed solvents (λex = 400 nm), the legend represents the polarity parameter of mixtures; (i) The relationship between solvent polarity parameter and IG/IRhB.
(a) H2O contents dependent IG/IRhB ratios of CDs-RhB, (b) typical PL spectra normalized by CDs peak intensity, or (c) RhB peak intensity.
(a), (b) UV absorption spectra of CDs-RhB in H2O/EtOH mixture (water percentages are labeled); (c), (d) Plots of (αhν)1/2 versus hν of CDs-RhB in the mixtures.

(A) The relationship between different percentages of water in ethanol and the two peak intensity ratios of CDs-RhB (a–d: 100%, 75%, 30%, 0, respectively). (B) Optical images (λex = 355 nm, indexes h and l represent the strips has been stored at environmental temperatures of 60 °C and 4 °C, respectively, for 2 days.) and (C) CIE color coordinates (λex = 400 nm, the red line in the upper right illustration represents the detection range) of CDs-RhB in different solvents. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the preparation of CDs-RhB.
Schematic illustration of the structure of CDs-RhB.
Hansen solubility parameter of selected solvents.
Fluorescence lifetime of CDs and CDs-RhB in water.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric dual-emission of Rhodamine-B grafted carbon dots for full-range solvent components detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Carbon Materials, Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 27 Taoyuan South Road, Taiyuan, 030001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Llaboratory of Carbon Materials, Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 27 Taoyuan South Road, Taiyuan 030001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Materials Science and Optoelectronics Engineering, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Materials Science and Optoelectronics Engineering
University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Materials Science and Optoelectronics Engineering, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, 19 Yuquan Road, Beijing, 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, 19 Yuquan Road, Beijing 100049, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Key Laboratory of Carbon Materials, Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 27 Taoyuan South Road, Taiyuan, 030001, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quick and visual detection of component contents, such as water, in a mixed solvent is important for many practical applications, and a full range detection is especially preferred. In this work, a carbon dots based ratiometric fluorescent sensor was synthesized by grafting fluorescent group (Rhodamine B, RhB) on carbon dots, and the dual emission peaks exhibited a linear ratiometric response with the change of polarity and hydrogen bond of Solvent Hansen solubility parameters. This responsive behavior is attributed to surface state photoluminescence mechanisms, and has been used for the quantitative detection of water content in ethanol with an excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.996), a low detection limit (0.2%), and a full detection range (0–100%). Furthermore, a paper-based ratiometric fluorescence sensing strip is also demonstrated, which exhibits good storage stability and sensitivity. This study suggests that RhB grafted carbon dots could be feasibly and effectively used as ratiometric fluorescent sensors for solvent content detection.

Selectively grafted carbon dots are developed for the ratiometric sensing of solvent properties and full range detection of water contents.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to unique photo-luminescence (PL) property, low toxicity, and excellent water solubility, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted great attentions in many application fields [1–3], such as bioimaging [4–6], light emitting [7–9], photo catalysing [9–11], and sensing [12–14]. The luminescence behavior of CDs is influenced by multiple factors in a comprehensive way, including (1) internal factors dominated emission, including conjugation effect, surface state, synergistic effect, and so on; (2) external factors dominated emission, such as molecular state and environment effect; (3) crosslink-enhanced emission [15].

The responsive PL behaviors of CDs on environmental factors including solvent type, temperature, pressure, and so on, could be used for detector or sensor. Many literatures have revealed that the solvent polarity affects both absorption and emission spectral properties of CDs [16], and the CDs emission light shifts to longer wavelength with the increase of solvent polarity [17–20]. However, it has to be pointed out that the broad emission peak and relatively small wavelength change along with solvent polarity changes lead to a low sensing sensitivity of CDs. By comparison with peak position shift, the emission intensity change would be more sensitive, but there are few reports on the influence of solvent polarity. Recently, using surface functionalization, CDs have been endowed with molecular recognition ability and are applied in chemical sensing [21]. Most researches focus on tuning the PL behaviors of the bulky of CDs, and there are few reports on fluorescence group functionalized CDs. By properly designing the grafted groups, the grated fluorescence groups on CDs could possibly introduce different or even contrary PL behaviors as compared with the bulky parts of CDs. For environmental factor ratiometric fluorescent (RF) sensor, comparative dual PL responses would provide much higher sensitivity and less error than single PL response. RF technology can not only eliminates many negative factors and helps to acquire more precise results, but also provides a visualization capability [22,23]. Additionally, since most fluorescence groups are toxic to live bodies, the tethering effect of CDs makes the fluorescence groups non-toxic.

In this work, glucose-based CDs are synthesized whose surface has many oxygen containing functional groups including phenolic hydroxyl, carboxyl, and etc. Then, the properly selected PL molecule, Rhodamine B (RhB), is chemically bonded on the surface of CDs. Interestingly, PL intensity and wavelength shift of CDs and RhB show opposite response on the change of solvent polarity, and could be used as a sensitive RF sensor to detect a full range of solvent mixture.

Glucose (99%) was purchased from Innochem Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). RhB (>99.0%), ethylenediamine (EDA, ≥99.5%), ethyl alcohol (EtOH, ≥99.5%), dipropylene glycol (DPG, 99%), triethylene glycol (TEG, >99.0%), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, ≥99.9%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. (China). Deionized water was produced by ultrapure water systems (Heal Force, Shanghai, China). Dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3500 Da) was obtained from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd.

As illustrated in Scheme 1 , 5 g glucose was dissolved in 45 mL deionized water, transferred into an autoclave, sealed, and kept at 180 °C for 12 h. Next, the autoclave was naturally cooled down to room temperature, and the solid and liquid products were separated by vacuum filtration. The CDs suspended in the liquid portion was further purified by dialysis, and then collected by freeze drying. For CDs surface modification, the CD dispersion was prepared under the same conditions; then, 3 g EDA and 0.6 g RhB were added subsequently, and the mixture was sealed into an autoclave. The reaction was carried out at 140 °C for 13 h. The product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, the corresponding centrifugation force was 10,620×g (g: gravitational acceleration). And the resulting supernatant was placed in a dialysis bag (M w cutoff: 3500 KDa) to remove small molecules. Finally, carbon dots (labeled as CDs-RhB) were freeze-dried to a dark brown power. The concentrations of CDs/CDs-RhB dispersions are 0.1 mg/mL unless specifically noted. For paper-based water sensors, a filtration paper was immersed in the CDs-RhB ethanol solution for 5 min then, the paper was dried in 50 °C oven. Finally, the filter paper soaked with CDs-RhB cut into a square strip with a dimension of 1 cm by 1 cm.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained on JEM-2100F and Tecnai G2 F20 S-Twin. UV–Vis absorption spectra were determined using a Shimadzu 3600 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra (PL) were performed with a HITACHI F-7000 Spectrophotometer. The ex- and em-slit are 10 nm unless otherwise noted. The Zeta potentials of the CDs/CDs-RhB were determined by a Malvern Zeta-sizer Nano. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded with a Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250 xi system using AL Kα radiation (1486.6 eV). Binding energies were calibrated with respect to C1s at 284.8 eV, and the fine spectra were measured at a resolution of ±0.05 eV. The Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopic (FTIR) studies were carried out using the FTIR Frontier (Bruker TENSORII, Germany) instrument. Fluorescence quantum efficiency and lifetime of CDs/CDs-RhB were recorded using an FLS1000 Edinburgh Analytical Instrument apparatus. For water concentration sensing, the original solution of the CDs-RhB (0.1 mg/mL) was prepared by dispersing CDs-RhB in anhydrous ethanol. Then, certain amount of water was added into CDs-RhB ethanol dispersion to tune water concentrations.

Since glucose is the precursor, the obtained CDs mostly contain C and O elements based on XPS results shown in Fig. 1 a. The C1s spectrogram (Fig. 1b) of CDs consists four fitting peaks at 284.6 eV, 285.8 eV, 287.0 eV, and 288.7 eV, namely, C–C, CC, C–O, and CO, respectively. The O1s spectrogram (Fig. 1c) consists two fitting peaks at 531.1 eV and 532.6 eV, namely, carbonyl group (CO), and hydroxyl or ester group (C–OH, C–O–C), respectively. For comparison, CDs modified with EDA alone were prepared, labeled as CDs-EDA. The XPS spectra of CDs-EDA are summarized in Fig. 1e–h. The abundant nitrogen atoms in EDA cause a significant increase of nitrogen elements on the surfaces of CDs from 0 to ∼10%. In the C1s fine spectrum of CDs-RhB (Fig. 1j), the fitting peaks at 284.4 eV, 285.4 eV, 286.7 eV, and 287.4 eV, are attributed to the carbons of CC, C–C, C–N/C–O, and CO/CN, respectively [24,25]. Comparing the O1s fine spectra of CDs and CDs-EDA, there is much less C–OH groups in CDs-EDA. Also, the oxygen content in CDs-EDA is ∼16%, about 40% less than CDs. These changes including element contents and bond types are due to the chemical reactions in between the carboxylic groups on CDs and ammonia groups of EDA, which consumes –OH bonds on CDs and introduces ammonia groups. Fig. 1h is the N1s fine spectrum of CDs-EDA. There are two fitting peaks at 399.5 eV and 400.8 eV belonging to pyrrolic-N and N–H, respectively. The ammonia groups of EDA would form pyrrolic-N after reacting with carboxylic groups on CDs or remain intact. The peak fitting results in Fig. 1h clearly show that the pyrrolic-N content is much higher than N–H, which suggests that more than half of the ammonia groups in EDA react. The XPS survey spectrum of CDs-RhB is shown in Fig. 1i. There are 15% nitrogen elements. The C1s and N1s fine spectra are shown in Fig. 1j and l, respectively. The N1s fitting peak at 398.4 eV is ascribed to pyridinic-N which exists in RhB. Comparing these spectra with CDs or CDs-EDA, the results indicate that RhB groups are grafted onto the CDs’ surfaces.

The FTIR spectra of CDs are plotted in Fig. 1d. For CDs, the stretching vibration peaks of O–H, CO, and C–O are located at 3353 cm−1, 1708 cm−1, and 1302 cm−1, respectively. For CDs-RhB, the double stretching vibration peaks of N–H are located at 3253 and 3397 cm−1, the stretching vibration peaks of CO, N–H in-plane bending vibration peaks, and C–N stretching vibration peaks are located at 1641 cm−1 (band Ⅰ), 1593 cm−1 (band Ⅱ), and 1443 cm−1 (band Ⅲ), respectively. These data indicate the formation of abundant amide bonds and oxygen-containing functional group in CDs-RhB, which is consistent with XPS results.

Fig. 2 a and e shows the TEM and HR-TEM images of CDs and CDs-RhB. These spherical CDs are uniformly dispersed. The HR-TEM images show lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.22–0.23 nm that agrees well with the (100) in-plane spacing of graphene [26]. The size distributions of CDs/CDs-RhB are statistically counted and the data is shown in Fig. 2b and f. The average diameters of CDs and CDs-RhB are 3.7 nm and 5.0 nm, respectively. After grafting with RhB, the diameter of carbon dots moderately increases. The UV absorbance spectra of CDs are shown in Fig. 2d. The absorption peak at 277 nm is from CDs, which derives from π-π∗ transition of aromatic sp2 domains; whereas, the peaks at 353 nm and 554 nm are ascribed to the n-π∗ transition of the heteroatomic bond (CO, CN) and RhB absorption in CDs-RhB, respectively. The zeta-potentials of CDs and CDs-RhB are shown in Fig. 2h. Due to the existence of abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on CDs’ surface, the zeta-potential is as low as −38.9 ± 1.73 mV. After grafting with EDA and RhB, the carboxyl groups on the surface are replaced by amino groups and the potential becomes positive, 6.91 ± 0.25 mV. Fig. 2c and g shows the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs and CDs-RhB excited at different laser wavelengths. The PL peak of CDs is located at 440 nm when excited at 320 nm. Along with the increase of excitation wavelength, the PL peak of CDs shifts to a higher wavelength, typical excitation dependent fluorescence behavior. By comparison, CDs-RhB exhibit two fluorescence peaks: the CDs peak (labeled as G) and RhB peak (labeled as RhB). Additionally, the fluorescence peak of CDs shifts from 440 nm to 470 nm when excited at 320 nm, and this shift is caused by the surface modification of EDA (Fig. S1 in ESI). Comparing excitation wavelength dependent PL behaviors of G and RhB peaks of CDs-RhB, the G peak shifts up at a higher excitation wavelength; whereas, the RhB peak barely changes. The excitation spectrum of CDs-RhB is shown in Fig. S2 in ESI. The excitation peak position is 398 nm, and the Stokes shift of CDs-RhB is 72 nm. The photographs of excited carbon dots dispersions are shown in the inset (bottom left) of Fig. 2a and e, the CDs and CDs-RhB dispersions emit strong blue and green fluorescence light, respectively, under 355 nm UV light. The relative emission intensity of G and RhB could be adjusted by the grafting contents of RhB. While the amount of RhB reactant decreases from 200% to 20% (based on EDA amount), the PL emission of RhB in CDs-RhB significantly weakens (Fig. S3 in ESI).

CDs-RhB contains two PL groups, namely, CDs and RhB groups. In order to reveal the solvent dependent PL behaviors of the two groups, various types of solvents and their mixtures are used. Here, five representative solvents are selected, and their Hansen solubility parameters are listed in Table 1 . The Hansen solubility parameters were taken from Hansen, C. M., Hansen Solubility Parameters-A User's Handbook. CRC Press: 2007 [27]. The Hansen solubility parameter contains three main portions: dispersion, polarity, and hydrogen bond. The dispersion force of these solvents has litter variations; therefore, polarity and hydrogen bond are considered as the primary factors. The parameters of the mixed solvent used are shown in Table S1 in ESI. The hydrogen bond and polarity of a solvent blend are approximately proportional to the volume fractions of the component solvents.

First, the influence of hydrogen bond on PL behaviors is studied. Water and DMSO shows intensive difference for hydrogen bond, while their polarities are close. To reveal the effect of hydrogen bond, the PL behaviors of CDs-RhB in the mixed solvents of water and DMSO are compared. The corresponding PL spectra are plotted in Fig. 3 a, b, and c. In water, the RhB peak is obvious as compared with G peak. Whereas, the RhB peak of CDs-RhB is very weak as compared with G peak in DMSO. It should be noted that the PL becomes stronger in DMSO than in water, and the comparison of G peak and RhB peak is relative. Here, an index, IG/IRhB, is defined as the intensity ratio of G peak over RhB peak. In the mixtures of water and DMSO, the relative intensity of G peak and RhB peak changes monotonously. The hydrogen bond dependent IG/IRhB plot is shown in Fig. 3d, and a linear dependence is observed with a slope of 0.022. Along with the increase of hydrogen bond, the ratio of IG/IRhB increases monotonously.

Second, the effect of solvent polarity on PL behavior is investigated. Fig. 3e, f, and g show the PL spectra of CDs-RhB in DPG, TEG and EtOH, respectively. It can be seen that the relative RhB peak intensity change significantly as the solvent polarity changes. The relative intensity of the PL peaks of G and RhB also depends on the agglomeration status of CDs-RhB. At a CDs-RhB concentration of less than 0.1 mg/mL, the emission peak positions and relatively intensities remain same; whereas, at a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL and higher, the G peak is greatly suppressed (Fig. S4 in ESI). The zeta-potential of CDs-RhB at different concentrations are shown in Fig. S5 in ESI. Zeta-potential decreases at a higher concentration, which suggests that CDs-RhB is not stable and aggregates at high concentrations [28]. The normalized PL spectra of CDs-RhB in mixed solvents, which are pairwise mixtures of these three solvents (DPG, TEG, and EtOH), are summarized in Fig. 3h, and the relative emission intensity of RhB increases along with the decrease of H2O/EtOH mixed solvent polarity. The solvent polarity dependent IG/IRhB ratios are plotted in Fig. 3i. Same with the effect of hydrogen bond, a linear relationship is found, and the corresponding slope is 0.111. The solvent polarity has a much stronger effect on the IG/IRhB ratio of CDs-RhB than solvent hydrogen bond.

The UV absorption spectra of CDs-RhB in different solvents are shown in Fig. S6 in ESI. The absence of two peaks at ca. 353 nm, 554 nm and peak shape variations of UV spectra in different solvents indicate that most excitation energy is consumed as heat by non-radiation decay through solvent-CDs-RhB interactions [29]. The PL spectra in Fig. 3a, b, f, and g are excitation-independent, since the surface states of CDs-RhB are the dominant factors controlling PL [30,31]. The PL originating from the surface state of CDs-RhB could be suppressed by the strong interaction between solvent and CDs-RhB, and the interaction itself could act as a predominant PL source. By comparison, the CDs-RhB in DPG shows an excitation wavelength-dependent behavior, since there are conformational changes induced by the swelling of CDs-RhB in DPG and the changes of proton donor-acceptor properties in the carbon core [32].

In order to better understand the effects of solvent parameters on the fluorescence intensity of CDs-RhB, the PL spectra of CDs and RhB dyes in various types of solvents are measured and summarized in Fig. S7 in ESI. The intensity of CDs increases with the increasing of the polarity (Fig. S7a), while the intensity of RhB is higher in lower polarity solvent (Fig. S7b). Also, the intensity of CDs is higher in a low hydrogen bond solvent, while RhB is opposite (Figs. S6c and d). These results are consistent with the PL emission behaviors of CDs-RhB in different solvents. The structure of CDs-RhB and its luminescence behaviors are summarized in Scheme 2 . According to XPS and FTIR, CDs are the nucleus of CDs-RhB, and its surface consists of abundant nitrogen/oxygen-containing groups and RhB molecules. These functional groups play important roles on the surface-state PL of carbon dots. Along with the enhancement of solvent polarity or hydrogen bond, the blue light of G peak is enhanced, the green light of RhB is weakened, and the overall CDs fluorescence changes from green color to blue color. While CDs-RhB are dispersed in H2O, the quantum efficiencies of G peak and RhB are 1.1% and 0.4%, respectively; by comparison, in ethanol, the quantum efficiency of G peak and RhB peak are 3.4% and 8.2%, respectively. This may be due to the strong solvation effect (interactions in between CDs-RhB and solvents) populates the radiative decay routes of G peak, and blocks the nonradiative decay pathways of RhB [19,33]. Sequentially, the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs were measured at an excitation laser wavelength of 375 nm (Fig. S8). The PL decay curves of CDs-RhB were detected by measuring the PL at 477 nm and 566 nm, respectively. The fluorescence decay of CDs and CDs-RhB fit well with the best bi-exponential and tri-exponential function, respectively. The lifetime (τ) and the fractional contributions of each component (A) as well as the average lifetime (τave) are summarized in Table 2 . Multiple emitting states are ascribed to the inhomogeneous properties of carbon dots [34]. The short lifetime PL (0–2 ns) is related to the intrinsic state of CDs and the middle fluorescence lifetime PL (2–4 ns) is ascribed to the surface state of CDs; by comparison, the long lifetime PL (7–11 ns) is due to RhB[35,36]. The PL lifetimes of CDs remain close before and after grafting with RhB, and the lifetime of RhB emission is much longer than CDs, which indicates that the PL emissions of CDs and the grafted RhB are mostly independent. When the detection wavelength increases from 477 nm to 566 nm, the average (τave) and the long fluorescence lifetime (τ3) of CDs-RhB both decrease, which indicate that the PL lifetime also depends on emission wavelength [37].

Based on the linear relationships in between solvent parameters and IG/IRhB ratios, the IG/IRhB ratio could be calculated based on the Hansen solubility parameters of a solvent; thus, the PL behaviors of CDs-RhB could be used as a rational sensor for detecting solvent components. Here, ethanol and water are mixed at various ratios, and the PL specra of CDs-RhB are measured. The measured IG/IRhB ratios are plotted in Fig. 4 a. The predicted curve is plotted in Fig. 4a based on the obtained slopes, 0.022 for hydrogen bond and 0.111 for polarity. The experimental data agree well with the predicted line. Fig. 4b and c summarize the typical PL spectra of the mixtures of water and ethanol. The numbers in Fig. 4b and c are the volume fraction of water in water/ethanol mixtures. When water content increases, both solvent polarity and hydrogen bond increases; the PL peak of RhB gradually shifts to higher wavelength (red shift), while the PL peak of G shifts to lower wavelength (blue shift). The PL positions of G and grafted RhB exhibit opposite response.

To explain the shifts of PL emission, the UV–vis absorption curves are characterized and summarized in Fig. 5 a. The absorbance peaks of RhB are magnified in Fig. 5b; the peak shifts to a higher wavelength at a higher water content, which is consistent with their PL spectra. The band-gaps of CDs-RhB in different H2O–EtOH mixtures are calculated based on the plots of (αhν)1/2 versus hν of CDs-RhB (Fig. 5c, and d). The calculated band gaps of carbon dots increase gradually along with the increase of water content, which leads to a PL hypochromatic shift. Since carbon dots act as proton donors in the H2O/EtOH binary solvent system, a blue shift occurs instead of a red shift in their PL spectra [38]. By comparison, the calculated band gaps of RhB decrease gradually along with the increase of water content, which lead to a PL bathochromic shift. As solvent polarity and hydrogen bond increase, the dipole moments have stronger effect on the surface electronic structures of the RhB, which leads to the decline of bandgaps and the corresponding red shifts of UV absorbance peaks and PL peaks [39]. It is noted that the peak position changes are much smaller than peak intensity changes.

In reality, the PL of carbon dots could possibly be affected by other factors, such as pH, metal ions, UV light illumination, and storage time. The PL spectra and IG/IRhB ratios of CDs-RhB measured at various pH values or with various metal ions in water are summarized in Fig. S9 in ESI. It is found that the PL peak position is relatively stable. But the IG/IRhB ratios increase when changing the pH or adding metal ions. In order to further explain the influence of metal ions in different solvents, Mg2+ (the maxima influence on IG/IRhB) and Cu2+ (the minimal influence on IG/IRhB) were selected to reveal their effects on the PL spectra of CDs-RhB in H2O/EtOH mixed solvent (Fig. S10 in ESI, the volume ratio is 1:1). The ratio of IG/IRhB decrease from 0.80 to 0.77 and 0.63 with the addition of Cu2+ and Mg2+, respectively. The ratiometric PL behaviors of CDs-RhB could be affected by metal ions. In addition, the fluorescence spectra of CDs-RhB before and after 355 nm UV light illumination in different solvent systems are shown in Fig. S11 in ESI. The IG/IRhB ratio in water slightly decreases after 12 h of UV irradiation, while remains same in H2O/EtOH mixture and EtOH, which indicates the carbon dots have a good resistance to UV irradiation. Regarding the colloidal stability, the PL spectra of CDs-RhB at 400 nm excitation after placing at room temperature for various days are compared. As can be seen from Fig. S12 in ESI, the IG/IRhB ratio barely changes even after 7 days indicating a good stability of CDs dispersion.

When excited at 400 nm, the IG/IRhB ratio of CDs-RhB increases when the water content in water/ethanol mixture increases. Based on experimental data, a linear experimental correlation (y = 1.390x+0.866, R2 = 0.996, in Fig. 6 A) could be obtained, where the ‘x’ is water content (%) and the ‘y’ is IG/IRhB. The limit of detection (LOD) is 0.2% based on the equation of LOD=(3σ/s), where σ is the standard deviation of black signals (n = 10) and s is the slope of the calibration curve [40]. To the best of our knowledge, the LOD is comparable or even lower to the most reported CDs [41–43]. More importantly, the linear dependency exists in a full detection range from 0% to 100% volumetric ratios. By comparison, the detected linear range of water content in ethanol as reported in the literatures is 0–5% and 5–20% [21], two linear regions of 0–40% and 40–100% [44], 0–1% [45,46], and 0–10% [47]. Our result indicate that CDs-RhB could be potentially utilized as an excellent optical sensor for the quantitative analysis of water in ethanol, and this method could be expanded to detect the components of other mixed solvents with polarity and hydrogen bond changes.

For convenience, a solid sensor strip is much favored than liquid dispersion. Based on the PL phenomena of CDs-RhB in liquid state, the same PL behavior could possibly be realized in solid state as well. This will meet more practical applications such as sensing and detection. The test strip is prepared by dipping the filter paper into CDs-RhB dispersion. The photographs of the wet test strips after dipped in 100% (a), 75% (b), 30% (c), and 0 (d) water-ethanol mixtures are shown in Fig. 6B (under the irradiation of 355 nm ultraviolet lamp), and the corresponding color coordinate points are shown in Fig. 6C. The detection range is shown in the upper right corner of Fig. 6C. It can be seen that the strips show blue color in water, green color in ethanol, and intermediate transition colors at different water/ethanol proportions. The paper-based sensing strip is easy handling, cost effective, and massively producible [44]. In addition, the sensitivity of the test strips to environmental temperatures is also compared. The test strips are stored in high temperature oven (60 °C) and low temperature refrigerator (4 °C) for two days before usage. The photos of the socked strips are shown in Fig. 6B, and no visible color change could be observed, indicating that the CDs has an excellent storage stability to a broad environmental temperature, which is of great value for practical usages.

In summary, glucose-based CDs are grafted of RhB by a two-step reaction method, and exhibit dual fluorescence emission behaviors from CDs and RhB, respectively. The PL of CDs and RhB show strong solvent dependent properties. The CDs-RhB exhibits ratiometric PL response with the Hansen solubility parameter of solvents. Along with the increase of solvent polarity and hydrogen bond, the intensity ratio of IG/IRhB linearly increases, and the slope of polarity is five times higher than that of hydrogen bond. Above ratiometric response is attributed to the surface-state PL mechanism. Additionally, the synthesized CDs-RhB has a good storage time stability, which provides the excellent requirements for ratiometric fluorescence sensor. In this work, the synthesized CDs-RhB have been used for detecting the content of water in ethanol; the CDs-RhB exhibit excellent linear ratiometric response with a low detection limit (0.2%) and a full detection range from 0 to 100%. Also, at a higher water content in ethanol, the CDs fluorescence peak shifts to smaller wavelength (blue shift) and RhB peak shifts to larger wavelength (red shift). Furthermore, a paper-based solvent sensing strip was prepared and applied to detect water content in alcohol drink, which proves the feasibility and effectiveness.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support by One Hundred Person Project of the Shanxi province, Science Foundation of Shanxi Province, China (Grant No. 201903D121006 and 201901D111006ZD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2021.338743.
